startScreenOptions.title: PY3.1-PY3.9 — Interactive Lecture (PY)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 6 embedded questions for PY3.1-PY3.9.

interactions.label: A neuron with a long axon requires a continuous supply of proteins and enzymes t

params.question: A neuron with a long axon requires a continuous supply of proteins and enzymes to maintain axonal function. Which component of the soma is primarily responsible for meeting this demand?

answers.text: Axon hillock — the trigger zone for action potential initiation

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The axon hillock is where action potentials are initiated, not where proteins are synthesised. It has a high density of voltage-gated sodium channels but no role in protein production.

answers.text: Nissl bodies — aggregates of rough endoplasmic reticulum

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Nissl bodies are the main site of protein synthesis in the neuron soma. They produce the structural and enzymatic proteins that are transported along the axon to maintain neuronal function.

answers.text: Myelin sheath — the insulating layer formed by glial cells

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The myelin sheath insulates the axon to speed conduction. It is produced by Schwann cells (PNS) or oligodendrocytes (CNS), not by the neuron's own soma.

answers.text: Synaptic boutons — the terminals that release neurotransmitter

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Synaptic boutons are the axon terminals where neurotransmitter release occurs. They themselves depend on proteins manufactured in the soma and transported down the axon.

overallFeedback.feedback: The correct answer is Nissl bodies. These are aggregates of rough endoplasmic reticulum responsible for protein synthesis. The axon hillock initiates action potentials, myelin insulates the axon, and synaptic boutons release neurotransmitters — none of these produce the proteins the neuron needs.

Correct. Nissl bodies (rough endoplasmic reticulum) are the main site of protein synthesis in the soma, producing the structural and enzymatic proteins transported along the axon to maintain neuronal function.

interactions.label: During the absolute refractory period following an action potential, no second a

params.question: During the absolute refractory period following an action potential, no second action potential can be generated regardless of stimulus strength. Which molecular event explains this?

answers.text: All voltage-gated potassium channels are maximally open, preventing any depolarisation

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: While potassium channels are open during repolarisation, this alone does not make the cell absolutely refractory. It is the inactivation of sodium channels that prevents a new action potential entirely.

answers.text: The sodium-potassium ATPase temporarily stops functioning

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase maintains long-term ion gradients but works too slowly to account for the millisecond-duration refractory period. The pump restores gradients over many cycles, not within a single action potential.

answers.text: Voltage-gated sodium channels are in an inactivated state and cannot reopen

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Voltage-gated sodium channels automatically enter an inactivated conformation about 0.5 ms after opening. This state cannot be reversed by depolarisation — only by returning to the resting potential, ensuring one-directional propagation.

answers.text: The membrane potential has exceeded the Nernst equilibrium potential for sodium

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The Nernst potential for sodium is approximately +60 mV. While the action potential peak approaches this, the refractory period is explained by channel inactivation — a conformational change — not by exceeding an equilibrium potential.

overallFeedback.feedback: The correct answer is sodium channel inactivation. About 0.5 ms after opening, voltage-gated sodium channels automatically enter an inactivated conformation that cannot be reopened by any stimulus. This ensures the action potential propagates in only one direction along the axon.

Correct. After opening, voltage-gated sodium channels enter an inactivated conformation that prevents reopening. Until they reset to the closed (but activatable) state, no stimulus can trigger another action potential — this is the molecular basis of the absolute refractory period.

metadata.title: During the absolute refractory period fo

interactions.label: Both botulinum toxin and tetanus toxin disrupt synaptic function by cleaving SNA

params.question: Both botulinum toxin and tetanus toxin disrupt synaptic function by cleaving SNARE proteins, yet they produce opposite clinical effects. Which statement best explains this difference?

answers.text: Botulinum toxin blocks calcium channels at the NMJ, while tetanus toxin blocks sodium channels in spinal motor neurons

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Neither toxin acts on ion channels. Both specifically cleave SNARE proteins required for vesicle fusion and neurotransmitter release — they differ in which synapses they target, not which channels they block.

answers.text: Botulinum toxin blocks acetylcholine release at the NMJ, while tetanus toxin blocks inhibitory neurotransmitter release in the spinal cord

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Botulinum toxin cleaves SNARE proteins at the neuromuscular junction, preventing acetylcholine release and causing flaccid paralysis. Tetanus toxin cleaves SNARE proteins at inhibitory interneurons, blocking GABA and glycine release, leaving excitation unopposed and causing spastic paralysis.

answers.text: Both toxins block acetylcholine, but at motor nerve terminals versus sensory nerve terminals

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Tetanus toxin does not block acetylcholine. It specifically targets inhibitory neurotransmitter release (GABA and glycine) in the spinal cord, which is why it causes spastic paralysis from unopposed excitatory drive.

answers.text: Botulinum toxin destroys nicotinic receptors, while tetanus toxin destroys GABA receptors

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Neither toxin acts on postsynaptic receptors. Both act presynaptically by cleaving SNARE proteins essential for vesicle fusion. The difference lies in which synapses they reach — peripheral NMJ for botulinum, central inhibitory synapses for tetanus.

overallFeedback.feedback: Both toxins target SNARE proteins but at different locations. Botulinum toxin cleaves SNAREs at the NMJ, preventing acetylcholine release — the muscle receives no signal and becomes flaccid. Tetanus toxin cleaves SNAREs at inhibitory interneurons in the spinal cord, blocking GABA and glycine release — excitation goes unopposed, causing spastic paralysis.

Correct. Both toxins cleave SNARE proteins, but at different synapses. Botulinum toxin acts at the NMJ, blocking acetylcholine release (flaccid paralysis). Tetanus toxin acts at inhibitory synapses in the spinal cord, blocking GABA and glycine release (spastic paralysis from unopposed excitation).

metadata.title: Both botulinum toxin and tetanus toxin d

interactions.label: After death, skeletal muscles become rigid — a condition known as rigor mortis.

params.question: After death, skeletal muscles become rigid — a condition known as rigor mortis. Based on the cross-bridge cycle, which specific step fails when ATP is depleted?

answers.text: Calcium cannot bind troponin C, so tropomyosin remains in the blocking position

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Calcium binding to troponin C does not require ATP directly. In fact, calcium levels rise after death because the SERCA pump (which does require ATP) can no longer remove calcium from the cytoplasm.

answers.text: Myosin heads cannot detach from actin, locking all cross-bridges in place

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. In the normal cross-bridge cycle, a new ATP molecule must bind the myosin head to release it from actin. Without ATP, all myosin heads remain firmly attached, locking every cross-bridge and producing the rigidity of rigor mortis.

answers.text: The power stroke cannot occur because ATP is needed to drive the myosin head pivot

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The power stroke is driven by the release of inorganic phosphate and ADP that were already bound to the myosin head. ATP hydrolysis re-cocks the head for the next cycle, but the critical failure in rigor mortis is the inability to detach.

answers.text: The sarcoplasmic reticulum cannot release calcium without ATP

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum occurs through ryanodine receptors triggered by the DHP receptor — this is a passive process. It is the SERCA pump that needs ATP, and its failure actually causes calcium to remain elevated, maintaining activation.

overallFeedback.feedback: The correct answer is that myosin heads cannot detach from actin. In the cross-bridge cycle, ATP must bind the myosin head to release it from actin. Without ATP, all cross-bridges remain locked in the attached state — this is rigor mortis, which resolves after about 48 hours as proteins degrade.

Correct. ATP binding to the myosin head is essential for detaching it from actin. When ATP is depleted after death, myosin heads remain permanently bound, locking all cross-bridges. Rigor mortis resolves after about 48 hours as muscle proteins degrade.

metadata.title: After death, skeletal muscles become rig

interactions.label: Individual muscle fibres follow the all-or-none law, yet a whole muscle can prod

params.question: Individual muscle fibres follow the all-or-none law, yet a whole muscle can produce smoothly graded force. Which pair of mechanisms allows the body to achieve this?

answers.text: Varying action potential amplitude in each fibre, and altering the calcium sensitivity of troponin

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Action potentials are all-or-none — their amplitude does not vary with stimulus strength. Force gradation depends on how many motor units are active and how frequently they are stimulated, not on modulating individual action potentials.

answers.text: Motor unit recruitment following the size principle, and increasing stimulation frequency to produce wave summation

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Henneman's size principle recruits small motor units first for fine control, adding larger units for greater force. Simultaneously, increasing stimulation frequency summates twitches — progressing from unfused to complete tetanus at about four times single-twitch tension.

answers.text: Switching between isometric and isotonic contraction modes, and adjusting the sarcomere resting length

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Isometric and isotonic describe the type of contraction, not a mechanism for grading force. While the length-tension relationship affects maximal force, the body grades force primarily through recruitment and frequency modulation.

answers.text: Increasing the number of cross-bridges per sarcomere, and prolonging the absolute refractory period

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The number of cross-bridges per sarcomere is fixed by filament overlap. The refractory period is an intrinsic property of the membrane and cannot be deliberately prolonged to increase force.

overallFeedback.feedback: The two key mechanisms are motor unit recruitment (Henneman's size principle — small units activated first, larger units added progressively) and stimulation frequency (higher frequencies cause wave summation, eventually reaching complete tetanus at about four times single-twitch tension).

Correct. The body grades muscle force through two mechanisms: motor unit recruitment (small units first, then larger — Henneman's size principle) and stimulation frequency (wave summation progressing to tetanus). Together, these produce smooth, variable force from all-or-none fibres.

interactions.label: Anaerobic glycolysis produces 36 to 38 ATP molecules per glucose molecule, makin

params.question: Anaerobic glycolysis produces 36 to 38 ATP molecules per glucose molecule, making it the primary energy source during sustained exercise beyond two minutes.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: This statement is false. Anaerobic glycolysis produces only 2 ATP per glucose — not 36–38. That higher yield belongs to aerobic oxidative phosphorylation in mitochondria, which requires oxygen and dominates beyond two minutes. Anaerobic glycolysis operates from about 10 seconds to 2 minutes, producing lactate as a by-product.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct — this statement is false. Anaerobic glycolysis yields only 2 ATP per glucose molecule. The 36–38 ATP figure belongs to aerobic oxidative phosphorylation, which uses the TCA cycle and electron transport chain in mitochondria and dominates energy supply beyond two minutes of exercise.
